title: Amazon EKS Kubernetes cluster scan detection
spl:
`aws_cloudwatchlogs_eks` "user.username"="system:anonymous" userAgent!="AWS Security Scanner"
      | rename sourceIPs{} as src
      | stats count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(responseStatus.reason) values(source) as cluster_name values(responseStatus.code) values(userAgent) as http_user_agent values(verb) values(requestURI)
        BY src user.username user.groups{}
      | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
      | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
      | `amazon_eks_kubernetes_cluster_scan_detection_filter`
description: The following analytic detects unauthenticated requests to an Amazon EKS Kubernetes cluster, specifically identifying actions by the "system:anonymous" user. It leverages AWS CloudWatch Logs data, focusing on user agents and authentication details. This activity is significant as it may indicate unauthorized scanning or probing of the Kubernetes cluster, which could be a precursor to an attack. If confirmed malicious, this could lead to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or disruption of se…

implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Various ingested and CIM-normalized.
knownFalsePositives: Not all unauthenticated requests are malicious, but frequency, UA and source IPs will provide context.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"Amazon EKS Kubernetes cluster scan detection" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Various. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1526. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "Amazon EKS Kubernetes cluster scan detection" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: Not all unauthenticated requests are malicious, but frequency, UA and source IPs will provide context.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

Confirm the required data sources are flowing:

```spl
| tstats count where index=* by index, sourcetype | sort -count
```

Verify the detection is enabled and firing:

```spl
index=_audit action="alert_fired" ss_name="*"
| stats count by ss_name, trigger_time | sort -trigger_time
```
grandmaExplanation: The following analytic detects unauthenticated requests to an Amazon clusters Kubernetes cluster, specifically identifying actions by the "system:anonymous" user, and we use Splunk so the team can see it early and act.
